sn55.54:1.1: On one occasion the Blessed One was staying among the Sakyans at Kapilavatthu in the Banyan Park.
sn55.54:1.2: Now at that time many monks were at work making robes for the Blessed One, (thinking,)
sn55.54:1.3: “When the robes are finished, at the end of the three months, the Blessed One will set out wandering.”
sn55.54:1.5: that many monks were at work making robes for the Blessed One, (thinking,)
sn55.54:1.6: “When the robes are finished, at the end of the three months, the Blessed One will set out wandering.”
sn55.54:1.7: So he approached the Blessed One and, on arrival, having bowed down, sat to one side. As he was sitting there he said to the Blessed One:
sn55.54:1.9: that many monks are at work making robes for the Blessed One, (thinking,)
sn55.54:1.10: ‘When the robes are finished, at the end of the three months, the Blessed One will set out wandering.’
sn55.54:1.11: But I haven’t heard in the Blessed One’s presence, haven’t learned in the Blessed One’s presence,
sn55.54:1.12: how a discerning lay follower who is diseased, in pain, severely ill should be instructed by (another) discerning lay follower.”
sn55.54:2.1: “Mahānāma, a discerning lay follower who is diseased, in pain, severely ill should be reassured by another discerning lay follower with four reassurances:
sn55.54:2.3: that you are endowed with verified confidence in the Awakened One: “Indeed, the Blessed One is worthy & rightly self-awakened, consummate in clear-knowing & conduct, well-gone, an expert with regard to the cosmos, unexcelled trainer of people fit to be tamed, teacher of devas & human beings, awakened, blessed.”
sn55.54:2.5: that you have verified confidence in the Dhamma: “The Dhamma is well taught by the Blessed One, to be seen here & now, timeless, inviting verification, pertinent, to be experienced by the observant for themselves.”
sn55.54:2.6: “‘Be reassured, friend, that you have verified confidence in the Saṅgha: “The Saṅgha of the Blessed One's disciples who have practiced well… who have practiced straight-forwardly… who have practiced methodically… who have practiced masterfully—in other words, the four pairs, the eight individuals—they are the Saṅgha of the Blessed One's disciples: deserving of gifts, deserving of hospitality, deserving of offerings, deserving of respect, the incomparable field of merit for the world.”
sn55.54:2.7: “‘Be reassured, friend, that you have virtues that are appealing to the noble ones: untorn, unbroken, unspotted, unsplattered, liberating, praised by the observant, ungrasped at, leading to concentration.’
sn55.54:3.1: “Mahānāma, when a discerning lay follower who is diseased, in pain, severely ill has been reassured by another discerning lay follower with these four reassurances, he should be asked:
sn55.54:3.2: ‘Friend, are you concerned for your mother & father?’
sn55.54:3.4: ‘I am concerned for my mother & father,’ he should be told,
sn55.54:3.5: ‘You, my dear friend, are subject to death.
sn55.54:3.6: If you feel concern for your mother & father, you’re still going to die.
sn55.54:3.7: If you don’t feel concern for your mother & father, you’re still going to die.
sn55.54:3.8: It would be good if you abandoned concern for your mother & father.’
sn55.54:4.2: ‘My concern for my mother & father has been abandoned,’ he should be asked,
sn55.54:4.3: ‘Friend, are you concerned for your wife & children?’
sn55.54:4.5: ‘I am concerned for my wife & children,’ he should be told,
sn55.54:4.6: ‘You, my dear friend, are subject to death.
sn55.54:4.7: If you feel concern for your wife & children, you’re still going to die.
sn55.54:4.8: If you don’t feel concern for your wife & children, you’re still going to die.
sn55.54:4.9: It would be good if you abandoned concern for your wife & children.’
sn55.54:5.2: ‘My concern for my wife & children has been abandoned,’ he should be asked,
sn55.54:5.3: ‘Friend, are you concerned for the five strings of human sensuality?’
sn55.54:5.5: ‘I am concerned for the five strings of human sensuality,’ he should be told,
sn55.54:5.6: ‘Friend, divine sensual pleasures are more splendid & more refined than human sensual pleasures.
sn55.54:5.7: It would be good if, having raised your mind above human sensual pleasures, you set it on the Devas of the Four Great Kings.’
sn55.54:6.2: ‘My mind is raised above human sensual pleasures and is set on the Devas of the Four Great Kings,’ he should be told,
sn55.54:6.3: ‘Friend, the Devas of the Thirty-three are more splendid & more refined than the Devas of the Four Great Kings.
sn55.54:6.4: It would be good if, having raised your mind above the Devas of the Four Great Kings, you set it on the Devas of the Thirty-three.’
sn55.54:7.2: ‘My mind is raised above the Devas of the Four Great Kings and is set on the Devas of the Thirty-three,’ he should be told,
sn55.54:7.3: ‘Friend, the Devas of the Hours are more splendid & more refined than the Devas of the Thirty-three. It would be good if, having raised your mind above the Devas of the Thirty-three, you set it on the Devas of the Hours.’ “If he should say, ‘My mind is raised above the Devas of the Thirty-three and is set on the Devas of the Hours,’ he should be told,
sn55.54:7.4: ‘Friend, the Contented Devas are more splendid & more refined than the Devas of the Hours…
sn55.54:7.5: the Devas Delighting in Creation are more splendid & more refined than the Contented Devas…
sn55.54:7.6: the Devas [Muses?] Wielding Power over the Creations of Others are more splendid & more refined than the Devas Delighting in Creation…
sn55.54:7.7: the Brahmā world is more splendid and more refined than the Devas Wielding Power over the Creations of Others.
sn55.54:7.8: It would be good if, having raised your mind above the Devas Wielding Power over the Creations of Others, you set it on the Brahmā world.’
sn55.54:7.10: ‘My mind is raised above the Devas Wielding Power over the Creations of Others and is set on the Brahmā world,’ he should be told,
sn55.54:7.11: ‘Friend, even the Brahmā world is inconstant, impermanent, included in self-identity.
sn55.54:7.12: It would be good if, having raised your mind above the Brahmā world, you brought it to the cessation of self-identity.’
sn55.54:8.2: ‘My mind is raised above the Brahmā world and is brought to the cessation of self-identity,’
sn55.54:8.3: then, I tell you, Mahānāma, there is no difference—in terms of release—between the release of that lay follower whose mind is released and the release of a monk whose mind is released.”
